title: Feudal America: Elements of the Middle Ages in Contemporary Society By Vladimir Shlapentokh and Joshua Woods Penn State Press, 2011. 184 pages. $54.95 cloth, $39.95 paper
abstract: The subtitle gives a more accurate view of the authors' argument than does the title. This book does not claim or demonstrate that the United States now, or in the past, is a feudal society. Rather, the authors contend that all societies contain feudal, liberal and authoritarian elements and that a successful analysis requires a “segmented approach” that can get at America's “deeply fragmented cultural and social patterns” (6). What do the authors mean by feudal? It is in part a residual category, encompassing social forms and behaviors that do not fit within the norms of liberal (i.e., democratic, market) society or the coercive power of authoritarian social organization. Thus, for Shlapentokh and Woods, feudalism is present wherever there is (1. evidence of state weakness, (2. “collusion and conflicts between the central administration and major autonomous social actors possessing political and economic power,” (3. corruption in large organizations, (4. “the use of personal relations in political and economic life, (5. the prevalence of elitist ideology and (6. the use of private agents and organizations for providing security and protection.”
